reasoning:
The finding is FALSE. The core claim that wp_kses with 'href => array()' permits javascript: or data: URIs is incorrect. WordPress's wp_kses applies wp_kses_bad_protocol() to href because href appears in wp_kses_uri_attributes() — a hardcoded list of 17 URI attributes that always receive protocol filtering regardless of the per-attribute allowlist value. javascript: and data: are not in WordPress's default allowed protocols ($allowedprotocols), so those schemes are stripped before the value is output. The <link href=javascript:...> and <link href=data:...> attack paths claimed in the finding are therefore blocked by wp_kses itself.

The secondary meta http-equiv injection angle is also blocked: the allowlist in add_favicon_markups() permits 'meta' with attributes name, property, and content — but NOT http-equiv. wp_kses strips any attribute not in the per-element allowlist, so a <meta http-equiv="refresh" content="..."> payload would have http-equiv stripped, leaving only a harmless <meta content="..."> tag.

Reachability: fbrfg_html_code is written in two places: (1) install_new_favicon(), which requires admin-level manage_options capability plus a valid WP nonce (double-verified including validating the API response's custom_parameter against the nonce), making this admin-only by design and not a privilege-escalation issue; and (2) the automatic update path check_for_updates() which fetches from https://realfavicongenerator.net — a network-level MitM of HTTPS traffic is required and is not a realistic WordPress plugin attack surface.

Even if an attacker somehow controlled the fbrfg_html_code option value, wp_kses would sanitize all claimed payloads before echo. The finding incorrectly assumes wp_kses('href' => array()) provides no URL sanitization; in fact href is a URI attribute and receives full protocol filtering.
